fr:mainmatter.html:p: The network connecting cores, L2/L3 banks, and memory controllers on a Multicore chip. The same abstractions that govern off-chip networks (topology, routing, flow control) apply, but the constraints are different: wires are abundant but die area is scarce, latency is dominated by hops rather than propagation, and the network is part of the Cache coherence critical path.
The measurements used to compare network topologies and traffic patterns.

html:ul.html:li: Degree: number of links at each node. A proxy for router complexity — each port costs a buffer, crossbar input, state machine.

Bisection bandwidth: bandwidth across a cut that splits the network into two equal-sized halves. The minimum over all such cuts. A proxy for peak achievable throughput, especially for uniformly random traffic.
Diameter: maximum hop distance between any two nodes. The worst-case hop count.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Bisection bandwidth is less useful for on-chip networks than off-chip because global on-chip wiring is plentiful.
Remark. Ring bisection is 2 (two cuts to bisect); torus bisection is 4.

html:ul.html:li: Hop count: hops between two specific nodes. The average over a traffic distribution is a useful proxy for latency.
Maximum channel load: the most-loaded link's load relative to per-node injection bandwidth, for a given traffic pattern. The proxy for sustained throughput.
Path diversity: number of distinct shortest paths between two nodes. Higher path diversity allows adaptive routing to spread load.
Zero-load latency: average hop count with no contention.
Saturation throughput: the injection rate at which latency curves upward exponentially.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Hop count is not the same as latency. Per-hop pipeline depth and link propagation matter — more short hops can beat fewer slow ones. Router pipeline depth isn't always known in advance.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Two organizational families for on-chip networks.

html:ul.html:li: Direct — every terminal node (core, memory controller) is associated with a router. Most on-chip designs are direct because area is precious and there's no point spending a router with no compute attached. The "k-ary n-cube" family (Ring topology, Mesh topology, Torus topology) is direct.

Indirect — routers may exist that connect only to other routers, with no terminal attached. Butterfly network and Clos network are indirect; a router in their middle stages just shuffles bits between input and output stages.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Indirect networks can shorten the diameter for the same node count, but they pay for extra silicon area for routers that don't serve traffic locally. Often the right call off-chip; usually the wrong call on-chip.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The simplest indirect network: an n \times m grid of switch points connecting n inputs to m outputs. Any input can be connected to any free output simultaneously — non-blocking.

html:ul.html:li: One hop per transit (minimum possible).
Serializes if multiple senders target the same receiver.
Switch area grows as O(n \cdot m) — the dominant cost as systems grow.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Crossbar router area scales as the square of the port count, which is why high-radix routers are dramatically more expensive than low-radix ones.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Crossbars are common as the inner switch of larger structured networks (Clos network, Fat tree) — large flat crossbars are impractical at scale, but small crossbars are cheap.

A direct network where each node connects to two neighbors, forming a closed loop. Bidirectional rings allow either direction of travel; unidirectional rings allow only one. The simplest topology to implement and reason about.
In the k-ary n-cube family, a ring is a k-ary 1-cube.

html:ul.html:li: Degree 2 (every node).
Diameter \lfloor k/2 \rfloor for a bidirectional k-node ring.
Bisection bandwidth 2 — cut the ring at exactly two links.
Hardware overhead very low: routers have two ports.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. Worst-case hop count is O(k). Doesn't scale beyond a small number of nodes.
Remark. Despite the poor scaling, rings are common in commercial CPUs (Intel's L3 ring bus on Sandy/Ivy Bridge) because they're cheap and 8–16 cores is enough.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A 2D grid of nodes with neighbors connected on the four compass directions, no wrap-around. The dominant on-chip topology in commercial designs (Xeon Phi, recent server x86 chips) because it lays out naturally on a 2D die.

html:ul.html:li: Interior nodes degree 4; edge nodes degree 3; corners degree 2.
Average hop count for an even-k mesh: H_{avg} = \frac {nk}{3}
Maximum channel load for uniform random traffic: k/4 — twice that of a Torus topology.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. Non-uniform node degree. Edge nodes carry less traffic than they could, while interior nodes bottleneck on traffic flowing through them.

Remark. Mesh dominates Torus topology in practice despite the topology metrics being worse, because torus wrap-around wires are physically long and force expensive folding to equalize link lengths. Manufacturing wins over theoretical performance.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A Mesh topology with wrap-around links connecting the ends of each dimension. In the k-ary n-cube classification, a k-ary n-cube. Uniform node degree (every node has degree 2n), uniform load distribution, half the hop count of a mesh.

html:ul.html:li: Every node has degree 2n.
Average hop count for even k: H_{avg} = \frac {nk}{4}
Maximum channel load for uniform random traffic: k/8 — half that of a Mesh topology.
Good path diversity: many shortest paths between any two nodes.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. Wrap-around links are physically long. To equalize wire delays, the torus must be folded, doubling effective wire lengths and link area. The wrap-around also doubles the link count versus a mesh of the same dimensions.
Remark. Despite the theoretical advantage, mesh wins on real chips. Tori show up in HPC interconnects (Cray) where the wires can run on dedicated cables.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Variants:

html:ul.html:li: k-ary n-cube: k nodes along each of n dimensions, all ends wrapped.
Asymmetric: (i, j, k)-ary — different node count per dimension.
Mesh: same as torus but without wrap-around.
Ring: k-ary 1-cube.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A multistage indirect network. A k-ary n-fly has k^n terminal nodes connected through n stages of k \times k crossbar switches. At each stage, the routing decision selects which of k sub-groups to enter; after n stages, every terminal can reach every other.

html:ul.html:li: Hop count n - 1.
Under uniform random traffic, maximum channel load is 1 (perfectly balanced).
Channel load drops further for non-uniform traffic that respects the natural partitioning.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. Zero path diversity. There is exactly one path between any source and destination. A single hot link wedges traffic.
Solution. Add shuffle stages that permute paths after each routing stage, so the same source-destination pair can be reached by multiple routes.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A three-stage indirect network parameterized by (m, n, r): r input switches and r output switches each of size n \times m, with m middle-stage switches each r \times r. Total terminal nodes N = nr. Non-blocking when m \geq 2n - 1.

html:ul.html:li: Number of total switching elements scales as N^{1.5} versus N^2 for a flat Crossbar while preserving non-blocking.
Path diversity is m — multiple distinct paths between any source and destination.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Proof. There are rmn input-switch elements, mr^2 middle-switch elements, and rmn output-switch elements: S = 2rmn + mr^2. With r = N/n: S = 2Nm + m(N/n)^2. Choosing n = \sqrt {N} and m \geq 2n - 1 = O(\sqrt {N}): S = 2mN + 2m\sqrt {N} = O(N^{1.5}).
Remark. A folded Clos network — input and output stages combined, making n input ports and m output ports bidirectional — is structurally the same as a Fat tree.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A tree topology with bandwidth that doubles at every level toward the root. Each interior switch has logical degree 4, but as you climb the tree, link width doubles to absorb the combined traffic of its subtree — preserving bisection bandwidth.

Structurally, a fat tree is a folded Clos network: the input and output stages of a Clos are merged into a single bidirectional stage at each level of the tree, and the middle stage becomes the higher levels of the tree.

html:ul.html:li: Each switch has logical degree 4 (2 down, 2 up — with link doubling).
Bisection bandwidth scales linearly with the number of leaves.
Path diversity grows toward the root.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Fat trees are the dominant topology for off-chip HPC interconnects (InfiniBand fat-tree clusters). Less common on-chip because the doubling links are hard to lay out on a 2D die.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Given a topology and a packet's source and destination, which path through the network does it take? Three classes of answer:

html:ul.html:li:
Greedy (shortest-path) — always pick the shortest available route.
(+) Minimizes latency under light load.
(-) Overloads central links; worst-case throughput poor.

Uniform / oblivious random — pick a direction at random.
(+) Spreads load; avoids systematic hotspots.
(-) Wastes bandwidth on non-minimal paths; latency unpredictable.

Adaptive — at each hop, pick the outgoing link with the lowest local congestion.
(+) Dynamically balances load; best worst-case throughput.
(-) Routers need congestion state; risk of livelock without care.

fr:mainmatter.html:div:
Example. Tornado traffic on an 8-node bidirectional ring: node i sends to (i+3) \bmod 8.
Greedy: all packets travel counter-clockwise. Bidirectional bandwidth wasted; \frac {1}{3} utilization.
Uniform: randomly clockwise/counter-clockwise. Counter-clockwise gets \frac {2}{3} utilization, clockwise \frac {2}{5}; worst-case \frac {2}{5}.
Adaptive: sends \frac {5}{8} counter-clockwise to match traffic exactly. Best.
Remark. Adaptive routing tends to need Network backpressure signaling to know where congestion is, and care to avoid livelock when many adaptive decisions chase each other.

Remark. Oblivious strategies like Dimension-order routing and Valiant's routing algorithm give deterministic guarantees (deadlock-free, bounded path length) at the cost of giving up the load-balancing of true adaptive routing.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: An oblivious Network routing strategies for k-ary n-cube networks (Mesh topology, Torus topology): always exhaust the offset in one dimension completely before changing to the next. E.g. in 2D mesh routing, route X-then-Y — never turn back to X after starting Y.

The dimension order prevents routing deadlock: cycles in the channel dependency graph are impossible because no packet ever requests a channel in a "lower" dimension once it has started using a higher one. The dependency graph is therefore acyclic, and the standard cycle-equals-deadlock argument applies.

html:ul.html:li: (+) Provably deadlock-free without virtual channels.
(-) Zero path diversity. Hot spots can't be routed around.
(-) Unaware of current network state — oblivious.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The standard remedy for the path-diversity problem is Valiant's routing algorithm, which keeps the deadlock-freedom of dimension-order routing while spreading load with a randomized intermediate hop.
An oblivious Network routing strategies that achieves provably optimal worst-case throughput by introducing randomness in path selection:

html:ol.html:li: For a packet going from s to d, randomly pick an intermediate node w.
Route s \to w using Dimension-order routing.
Eject the packet at w, then re-inject it.
Route w \to d using Dimension-order routing.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: To preserve deadlock-freedom, the intermediate node must remove the packet from the network before re-injecting — otherwise the channel dependencies of the two halves could interlock.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Restricting w to lie in the sub-quadrant defined by s and d keeps paths near-minimal while still spreading load — the practical compromise between guaranteed throughput and added latency.

Remark. Valiant's algorithm trades average-case latency (paths are longer) for worst-case throughput (any traffic pattern looks roughly uniform once randomized). The right call for adversarial workloads; overkill for uniform traffic.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Routing deadlock occurs when a packet cannot make progress because the buffer at its next link is full, and the packet holding that buffer is itself blocked waiting on a buffer this packet holds — a cycle in the channel dependency graph.
Two structural responses:

html:ol.html:li: Avoid deadlock by construction. Dimension-order routing enforces an ordering on dimension transitions so the dependency graph is acyclic by design.

Detect and recover from deadlock. Allow all turns. Detect cycles via timeouts. Drain stuck packets onto a backup network (a "slow channel" — physical, or more commonly an escape virtual channel) that uses dimension-order routing.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The relationship to other related concepts:

html:ul.html:li: Backpressure (Network backpressure) is the signaling mechanism that lets a router downstream tell upstream it is congested, before the upstream actually fills the link.
Adaptive routing avoids deadlock locally; backpressure lets adaptive routing react to remote congestion.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A signaling mechanism that lets a downstream router tell upstream "I am congested — slow down" before its buffers actually overflow. With backpressure information, adaptive routing can route around remote congestion, not just local.

The mechanism propagates: a congested link signals back to its sender, which then knows its next link will fill, which signals further back. Useful information flows in the opposite direction of the data.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. Acting only on local state can cause livelock — packets bounce between hotspots, never converging.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Backpressure breaks the livelock by giving local routers global-ish information without an actual global view.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Backpressure is mechanism; adaptive routing is policy. The two together are what real high-radix on-chip networks use.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The on-chip network connects each core (with its L1) and the L2 cache banks (which may be shared or private). Each L2 bank has a cache controller (manages Cache coherence) and, at memory boundaries, a memory controller (talks to DRAM).
Two cache-organization choices interact with the network design:

html:ul.html:li: Private L2 caches: each tile owns its L2 slice; cross-tile traffic happens only on private-L2 miss. (+) Lower hit latency; less interconnect traffic. (-) Replicated data; more off-chip traffic on misses.
Shared L2 cache: a single logical L2 split into banks, with each address mapped to a home bank. (+) Better utilization, no replication. (-) Every L1 miss goes on the network.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The interface to the network sits at the cache controller:

html:ul.html:li: Miss status handling register (MSHR): on a cache miss, records what request the cache is waiting on. Matched against the reply when it arrives.
Transaction status handling register (TSHR): tracks outstanding memory transactions to off-chip memory from the directory cache.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Memory controllers are commonly placed at the chip perimeter, near the I/O pads. A memory controller can share a router with a core/cache tile (saves area; needs arbitration) or have its own (more area; no contention).
Remark. Hardware-managed shared memory is one design point; another is explicit message passing, where software (not hardware) is responsible for moving data between processes.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Links. Thicker wires with larger spacing → lower resistance, higher speed, better energy efficiency. The wire budget determines how many ports you can afford per router.

Routers. Each port costs a buffer, a crossbar input, state logic, and a link to its neighbor. Crossbar area scales as the square of port count, so high-radix routers are dramatically expensive. Rings have very simple routers but poor performance.

Floorplan. Tori need to be folded to equalize wire lengths, doubling effective wire length; meshes don't. Tori also have twice as many links as meshes, which may force narrower links and lower per-link bandwidth.
Folding can also equalize path lengths inside a network at the cost of increasing average latency.
Concentration allocates more cores per router. (-) Decreases throughput per node. (+) Programs with high locality do little remote communication, so concentration is a good fit there.
General topologies aren't always optimal. Two construction strategies:

html:ul.html:li: Splitting: start with a large crossbar; split into smaller switches until constraints are met.
Merging: start with many small switches and combine.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Hierarchical topologies use one topology within clusters and another across clusters — common when traffic has locality.
The 15 most recently modified trees, by git commit time.
This will also serve as a bit of notes from A Primer on Memory Consistency and Cache Coherence
Syntactical things I need to remember from Murphi docs. Most things are straightforward, but there are a few things to note:
A declaration is a variable or a collection of variables that are used in the rules.
A rule is a transition from one state to another

html:ul.html:li: Each rule can optionally have an initial condition required to be fired
We can easily define rules for all the processors or collection of n things with ruleset.
For our use case, rules are used to define

html:ol.html:li: Controller actions
Receiving a request from the network
Retrying stalled requests

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A startstate is the intial configuration for all our declarations.

html:ul.html:li: Must assign a value to every global variable

fr:mainmatter.html:p: An invariant is something that Murphi checks to ensure is true at all possible configurations of the system.
Overview straight from the docs. Repeat forever:

html:ol.html:li: Find all rules whose conditions are true in the current state. (i.e. conditional expressions are true, given the current values of the global variables).
Choose one arbitrarily and execute the action, yielding a new state.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter:
The first thing we need to do is understand all the things that can go awry.
First in the valid-invalid protocol example we were given they only have two virtual channels. However, we need 3 channels to handle, request messages, forwarded messages, and response messages.
Let us briefly understand why this is the case. Detecting a deadlock is equivalent to detecting a cycle in a dependency graph, so it is sufficient to show a cyclic dependency.
If we issue a GetS this goes to the directory, giving us an edge N1 -> D.
Then the directory issues a Fwd-GetS to the ownder, giving us the chain N1 -> D -> N2
The owner then needs to send data to the requester, giving us the chain N1 -> D -> N2 -> N1.
Namely, the data must go through the same channel as all of the requests depending on it.
To avoid this, we partition into 3 channels:
Request: GetS, GetM, PutM
Forwarded: Fwd-GetS, Fwd-GetM, PutAck, Inv
Response: InvAck, Data
We directly enumerate these in our MessageType as MessageType: enum { GetS, -- request for shared copy GetM, -- request for modified (exclusive) copy PutS, -- evict shared copy PutM, -- evict modified copy (w/ data)
FwdGetS, -- forwarded GetS to owner FwdGetM, -- forwarded GetM to owner Inv, -- invalidation request
Data, -- data response InvAck, -- invalidation acknowledgment PutAck -- put acknowledgment }; Cache controller. We will start with MSI cache controller, given by the following table:
Notably, we are missing some of the messages in the figures below. We can handle these based on the AckCount ProcState: Record state: enum { P_Invalid, P_Shared, P_Modified, --stable states PT_IS_D, --sent GetS, waiting for Data PT_IS_DI, --sent GetS, got Inv, waiting for Data then go to I PT_IM_AD, --sent GetM (from I), waiting for Data and InvAcks PT_SM_AD, --sent GetM (from S), waiting for Data and InvAcks PT_IM_A, --got Data for GetM, waiting for remaining InvAcks PT_MI_A, --sent PutM, waiting for PutAck PT_SI_A, --sent PutS, waiting for PutAck PT_II_A --waiting for PutAck, already invalidated }; val: Value; ack: AckCount; -- tracks InvAck count End;
Remark. The load, store, and replacement all only alter the states of the coherence protocol for the permanent states. Therefore, to simulate the system it is sufficient to only implement the rules that initiate these state transitions i.e. evict in modified state or load from invalid state.
Directory controller. Now for the directory controller:
Remark. We actually end up adding a second transient state to the directory controller M^D. This is to handle the PutM, GetM race described below.
Weird Races.
Problem. When implementing the PutM rule to downgrade from modified to invalid. We must NOT get rid of the data until we have been acknowledged, so that we can still service forward requests. This is a transition to MI^A. However, we can still receive the PutAck causing us to transition to I before servicing the Fwd-GetM request. This is lost data.
Solution. We must add a M^D state to the directory controller. In this state, if we get a PutM request from a node that is not our current owner we stall and wait for data to arrive. For M^D to transition to M, we must send the data back to the directory even though we are doing a three-hop, so that the directory knows the data has also been forwarded to the new owner. It is unclear to me the best way to do this without this state.
Problem.
The above solution causes a deadlock, where two processors both store miss and one load misses. The "winning processor" of the store will be the second one. The first one will get the data from the directory transitioning from IM^{AD} \to IM^{A}, but the second one will then trigger a Fwd-GetM overwriting the first.
The load miss transitions to IS^D. However, when it receives an Inv it stalls according to the above figure. This blocks the Fwd-GetM from reaching the other winning processor because they are on the same VC. Thus, we have a deadlock where the owner never gets the data.
Solution. We need to add another transient state not included in the figure. Namely, we add II^{A} that says we were in IS^{A}, but we got invalidated and now we are waiting for our PutAck before we transition to I.
Remark. When the home directory is in M and we get a GetS we cannot clear owner as directed by figure. Otherwise in S^D, we will not be able to tell if the PutM is from the owner and stall, so we only remove the owner once we have received the data.

Aside from the modifications discussed above, the rest was straightforward and we now are ready to move on to MESI.
Cache controller. We will start with MESI cache controller, given by the following table:
Design choice 1. Since we allow immediate upgrade to M, without forwarding, if E upgrades to M, then the value in the directory sent to the next GetS / GetM would not have updated value. It seems we must either
not allow E to silently upgrade to M and then we do not need forwarding, or
allow E to silently upgrade to M and treat E as M basically and forward everything. Notably, neither of these have to write back on PutE, but we will go with 1 because that is how it is indicated in the figure.
Design Choice 2. In order to indicate that the directory received the first GetS we introduce a ExclusiveData message. Upon receiving, ExclusiveData in IS^D we transition to E.
Remark. Once we have sent the PutE, or the PutM EI^A and MI^A are functionally the same. I include both for completeness but verified that transitioning directly to MI^A from E works the same.
Directory controller. We start with the analagous table.
Similar to the cache controller, we also add a E^D state for completion that can be absorbed into M^D introduced in the MSI protocol.
Weird Races.
Problem. First processor does GetS and is in IS^D but the Home directory sends it exclusive data and is now in E. Then second processor does GetM and Home issues Fwd-GetM to processor one, which arrives before data.
Solution. Need to stall on Fwd-GetM and Fwd-GetS in IS^D until data arrives
Problem. An even crazier race happens when we get invalidated before the Fwd-GetM or Fwd-GetS arrive. Due to our previous design decision, we transition to II^A, where if we receive a PutAck we transition to invalid. This then loses the needed forward data.
Example. The timeline:
Proc_1 performs a load miss → IS^D
Home receives first GetS from Proc_1 → E and sends ExclusiveData to Proc_1
Proc_2 issues GetM → IM^AD
Home sends Inv to Proc_1
Home forwards Fwd-GetM to Proc_1
Proc_1 receives Inv → II^D
Proc_1 receives ExclusiveData → I Data is lost
Solution. We once again have to use the ExclusiveData message. We can then see that we got ExclusiveData in II^D and transition to E instead of I. E will then correctly handle the Fwd-GetM or Fwd-GetS.
We are left with the following state diagrams for the cache and directory controllers respectively

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The network connecting cores, L2/L3 banks, and memory controllers on a Multicore chip. The same abstractions that govern off-chip networks (topology, routing, flow control) apply, but the constraints are different: wires are abundant but die area is scarce, latency is dominated by hops rather than propagation, and the network is part of the Cache coherence critical path.
The measurements used to compare network topologies and traffic patterns.

html:ul.html:li: Degree: number of links at each node. A proxy for router complexity — each port costs a buffer, crossbar input, state machine.

Bisection bandwidth: bandwidth across a cut that splits the network into two equal-sized halves. The minimum over all such cuts. A proxy for peak achievable throughput, especially for uniformly random traffic.
Diameter: maximum hop distance between any two nodes. The worst-case hop count.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Bisection bandwidth is less useful for on-chip networks than off-chip because global on-chip wiring is plentiful.
Remark. Ring bisection is 2 (two cuts to bisect); torus bisection is 4.

html:ul.html:li: Hop count: hops between two specific nodes. The average over a traffic distribution is a useful proxy for latency.
Maximum channel load: the most-loaded link's load relative to per-node injection bandwidth, for a given traffic pattern. The proxy for sustained throughput.
Path diversity: number of distinct shortest paths between two nodes. Higher path diversity allows adaptive routing to spread load.
Zero-load latency: average hop count with no contention.
Saturation throughput: the injection rate at which latency curves upward exponentially.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Hop count is not the same as latency. Per-hop pipeline depth and link propagation matter — more short hops can beat fewer slow ones. Router pipeline depth isn't always known in advance.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Indirect networks can shorten the diameter for the same node count, but they pay for extra silicon area for routers that don't serve traffic locally. Often the right call off-chip; usually the wrong call on-chip.

html:ul.html:li: One hop per transit (minimum possible).
Serializes if multiple senders target the same receiver.
Switch area grows as O(n \cdot m) — the dominant cost as systems grow.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Crossbars are common as the inner switch of larger structured networks (Clos network, Fat tree) — large flat crossbars are impractical at scale, but small crossbars are cheap.

A direct network where each node connects to two neighbors, forming a closed loop. Bidirectional rings allow either direction of travel; unidirectional rings allow only one. The simplest topology to implement and reason about.
In the k-ary n-cube family, a ring is a k-ary 1-cube.

html:ul.html:li: Degree 2 (every node).
Diameter \lfloor k/2 \rfloor for a bidirectional k-node ring.
Bisection bandwidth 2 — cut the ring at exactly two links.
Hardware overhead very low: routers have two ports.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. Worst-case hop count is O(k). Doesn't scale beyond a small number of nodes.
Remark. Despite the poor scaling, rings are common in commercial CPUs (Intel's L3 ring bus on Sandy/Ivy Bridge) because they're cheap and 8–16 cores is enough.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A 2D grid of nodes with neighbors connected on the four compass directions, no wrap-around. The dominant on-chip topology in commercial designs (Xeon Phi, recent server x86 chips) because it lays out naturally on a 2D die.

html:ul.html:li: Interior nodes degree 4; edge nodes degree 3; corners degree 2.
Average hop count for an even-k mesh: H_{avg} = \frac {nk}{3}
Maximum channel load for uniform random traffic: k/4 — twice that of a Torus topology.

Remark. Mesh dominates Torus topology in practice despite the topology metrics being worse, because torus wrap-around wires are physically long and force expensive folding to equalize link lengths. Manufacturing wins over theoretical performance.

html:ul.html:li: Every node has degree 2n.
Average hop count for even k: H_{avg} = \frac {nk}{4}
Maximum channel load for uniform random traffic: k/8 — half that of a Mesh topology.
Good path diversity: many shortest paths between any two nodes.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. Wrap-around links are physically long. To equalize wire delays, the torus must be folded, doubling effective wire lengths and link area. The wrap-around also doubles the link count versus a mesh of the same dimensions.
Remark. Despite the theoretical advantage, mesh wins on real chips. Tori show up in HPC interconnects (Cray) where the wires can run on dedicated cables.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Variants:

html:ul.html:li: k-ary n-cube: k nodes along each of n dimensions, all ends wrapped.
Asymmetric: (i, j, k)-ary — different node count per dimension.
Mesh: same as torus but without wrap-around.
Ring: k-ary 1-cube.

html:ul.html:li: Hop count n - 1.
Under uniform random traffic, maximum channel load is 1 (perfectly balanced).
Channel load drops further for non-uniform traffic that respects the natural partitioning.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. Zero path diversity. There is exactly one path between any source and destination. A single hot link wedges traffic.
Solution. Add shuffle stages that permute paths after each routing stage, so the same source-destination pair can be reached by multiple routes.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A three-stage indirect network parameterized by (m, n, r): r input switches and r output switches each of size n \times m, with m middle-stage switches each r \times r. Total terminal nodes N = nr. Non-blocking when m \geq 2n - 1.

html:ul.html:li: Number of total switching elements scales as N^{1.5} versus N^2 for a flat Crossbar while preserving non-blocking.
Path diversity is m — multiple distinct paths between any source and destination.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Proof. There are rmn input-switch elements, mr^2 middle-switch elements, and rmn output-switch elements: S = 2rmn + mr^2. With r = N/n: S = 2Nm + m(N/n)^2. Choosing n = \sqrt {N} and m \geq 2n - 1 = O(\sqrt {N}): S = 2mN + 2m\sqrt {N} = O(N^{1.5}).
Remark. A folded Clos network — input and output stages combined, making n input ports and m output ports bidirectional — is structurally the same as a Fat tree.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A tree topology with bandwidth that doubles at every level toward the root. Each interior switch has logical degree 4, but as you climb the tree, link width doubles to absorb the combined traffic of its subtree — preserving bisection bandwidth.

Structurally, a fat tree is a folded Clos network: the input and output stages of a Clos are merged into a single bidirectional stage at each level of the tree, and the middle stage becomes the higher levels of the tree.

html:ul.html:li: Each switch has logical degree 4 (2 down, 2 up — with link doubling).
Bisection bandwidth scales linearly with the number of leaves.
Path diversity grows toward the root.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Fat trees are the dominant topology for off-chip HPC interconnects (InfiniBand fat-tree clusters). Less common on-chip because the doubling links are hard to lay out on a 2D die.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Given a topology and a packet's source and destination, which path through the network does it take? Three classes of answer:

html:ul.html:li:
Greedy (shortest-path) — always pick the shortest available route.
(+) Minimizes latency under light load.
(-) Overloads central links; worst-case throughput poor.

Uniform / oblivious random — pick a direction at random.
(+) Spreads load; avoids systematic hotspots.
(-) Wastes bandwidth on non-minimal paths; latency unpredictable.

Adaptive — at each hop, pick the outgoing link with the lowest local congestion.
(+) Dynamically balances load; best worst-case throughput.
(-) Routers need congestion state; risk of livelock without care.

fr:mainmatter.html:div:
Example. Tornado traffic on an 8-node bidirectional ring: node i sends to (i+3) \bmod 8.
Greedy: all packets travel counter-clockwise. Bidirectional bandwidth wasted; \frac {1}{3} utilization.
Uniform: randomly clockwise/counter-clockwise. Counter-clockwise gets \frac {2}{3} utilization, clockwise \frac {2}{5}; worst-case \frac {2}{5}.
Adaptive: sends \frac {5}{8} counter-clockwise to match traffic exactly. Best.
Remark. Adaptive routing tends to need Network backpressure signaling to know where congestion is, and care to avoid livelock when many adaptive decisions chase each other.

Remark. Oblivious strategies like Dimension-order routing and Valiant's routing algorithm give deterministic guarantees (deadlock-free, bounded path length) at the cost of giving up the load-balancing of true adaptive routing.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: An oblivious Network routing strategies for k-ary n-cube networks (Mesh topology, Torus topology): always exhaust the offset in one dimension completely before changing to the next. E.g. in 2D mesh routing, route X-then-Y — never turn back to X after starting Y.

The dimension order prevents routing deadlock: cycles in the channel dependency graph are impossible because no packet ever requests a channel in a "lower" dimension once it has started using a higher one. The dependency graph is therefore acyclic, and the standard cycle-equals-deadlock argument applies.

html:ul.html:li: (+) Provably deadlock-free without virtual channels.
(-) Zero path diversity. Hot spots can't be routed around.
(-) Unaware of current network state — oblivious.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The standard remedy for the path-diversity problem is Valiant's routing algorithm, which keeps the deadlock-freedom of dimension-order routing while spreading load with a randomized intermediate hop.
An oblivious Network routing strategies that achieves provably optimal worst-case throughput by introducing randomness in path selection:

html:ol.html:li: For a packet going from s to d, randomly pick an intermediate node w.
Route s \to w using Dimension-order routing.
Eject the packet at w, then re-inject it.
Route w \to d using Dimension-order routing.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: To preserve deadlock-freedom, the intermediate node must remove the packet from the network before re-injecting — otherwise the channel dependencies of the two halves could interlock.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Restricting w to lie in the sub-quadrant defined by s and d keeps paths near-minimal while still spreading load — the practical compromise between guaranteed throughput and added latency.

Remark. Valiant's algorithm trades average-case latency (paths are longer) for worst-case throughput (any traffic pattern looks roughly uniform once randomized). The right call for adversarial workloads; overkill for uniform traffic.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Routing deadlock occurs when a packet cannot make progress because the buffer at its next link is full, and the packet holding that buffer is itself blocked waiting on a buffer this packet holds — a cycle in the channel dependency graph.
Two structural responses:

html:ol.html:li: Avoid deadlock by construction. Dimension-order routing enforces an ordering on dimension transitions so the dependency graph is acyclic by design.

Detect and recover from deadlock. Allow all turns. Detect cycles via timeouts. Drain stuck packets onto a backup network (a "slow channel" — physical, or more commonly an escape virtual channel) that uses dimension-order routing.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The relationship to other related concepts:

html:ul.html:li: Backpressure (Network backpressure) is the signaling mechanism that lets a router downstream tell upstream it is congested, before the upstream actually fills the link.
Adaptive routing avoids deadlock locally; backpressure lets adaptive routing react to remote congestion.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A signaling mechanism that lets a downstream router tell upstream "I am congested — slow down" before its buffers actually overflow. With backpressure information, adaptive routing can route around remote congestion, not just local.

The mechanism propagates: a congested link signals back to its sender, which then knows its next link will fill, which signals further back. Useful information flows in the opposite direction of the data.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. Acting only on local state can cause livelock — packets bounce between hotspots, never converging.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Backpressure breaks the livelock by giving local routers global-ish information without an actual global view.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Backpressure is mechanism; adaptive routing is policy. The two together are what real high-radix on-chip networks use.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The on-chip network connects each core (with its L1) and the L2 cache banks (which may be shared or private). Each L2 bank has a cache controller (manages Cache coherence) and, at memory boundaries, a memory controller (talks to DRAM).
Two cache-organization choices interact with the network design:

html:ul.html:li: Private L2 caches: each tile owns its L2 slice; cross-tile traffic happens only on private-L2 miss. (+) Lower hit latency; less interconnect traffic. (-) Replicated data; more off-chip traffic on misses.
Shared L2 cache: a single logical L2 split into banks, with each address mapped to a home bank. (+) Better utilization, no replication. (-) Every L1 miss goes on the network.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The interface to the network sits at the cache controller:

html:ul.html:li: Miss status handling register (MSHR): on a cache miss, records what request the cache is waiting on. Matched against the reply when it arrives.
Transaction status handling register (TSHR): tracks outstanding memory transactions to off-chip memory from the directory cache.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Memory controllers are commonly placed at the chip perimeter, near the I/O pads. A memory controller can share a router with a core/cache tile (saves area; needs arbitration) or have its own (more area; no contention).
Remark. Hardware-managed shared memory is one design point; another is explicit message passing, where software (not hardware) is responsible for moving data between processes.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Links. Thicker wires with larger spacing → lower resistance, higher speed, better energy efficiency. The wire budget determines how many ports you can afford per router.

Routers. Each port costs a buffer, a crossbar input, state logic, and a link to its neighbor. Crossbar area scales as the square of port count, so high-radix routers are dramatically expensive. Rings have very simple routers but poor performance.

Floorplan. Tori need to be folded to equalize wire lengths, doubling effective wire length; meshes don't. Tori also have twice as many links as meshes, which may force narrower links and lower per-link bandwidth.
Folding can also equalize path lengths inside a network at the cost of increasing average latency.
Concentration allocates more cores per router. (-) Decreases throughput per node. (+) Programs with high locality do little remote communication, so concentration is a good fit there.
General topologies aren't always optimal. Two construction strategies:

html:ul.html:li: Splitting: start with a large crossbar; split into smaller switches until constraints are met.
Merging: start with many small switches and combine.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Hierarchical topologies use one topology within clusters and another across clusters — common when traffic has locality.
Collection of resources

html:ul.html:li: https://llvm.org/devmtg/2020-09/slides/MLIR_Tutorial.pdf
https://www.lei.chat/posts/mlir-linalg-dialect-and-patterns/
https://www.lei.chat/posts/mlir-vector-dialect-and-patterns/

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Dialects. This is effectively a syntax. For your given DSL, you must write each of the operations in this MLIR dialect in order to inherit the benefits of the MLIR ecosystem.
fr:mainmatter.html:blockquote:
The whole structured CodeGen paradigm in MLIR prefers co-designing op semantics and transformations and let ops structurally encode and guarantee certain properties to simplify analyses and transformations.
— Lei Zhang
fr:mainmatter.html:p: The general paradigm: It seems that when you are writing a DSL backed by MLIR that you

html:ol.html:li: Write an MLIR dialect for your DSL
You can write custom compiler passes for your MLIR dialect.

html:ul.html:li: This is what is done in Triton.
Triton has two custom MLIR dialects: ttir and ttgir.
The ttir dialect is the backend agnostic IR.
The ttgir dialect is the Triton GPU IR.

html:ol.html:li: Write a lowering pass from your MLIR dialect to the MLIR dialect of an MLIR with codegen backing i.e.

html:ul.html:li: MLIR has an LLVM dialect

Alternatively, XLA ingests StableHLO which is an MLIR dialect. In theory, this means that if your DSL is similar to that of a JAX / LinAlg dialect, then you can get a lot of the optimizations Google has come up with for free.

However, XLA works by translating StableHLO to HLO, which is then optimized by XLA. HLO is NOT an MLIR dialect and therefore in order to use XLA your backend needs to have an appropriate conversion from HLO to an executable on their backend.
This is what is done for trainium. Namely, the neuronx-cc compiler performs the translation from optimized HLO to optimized executable code on their ASIC.
Triton and the GPU backend for XLA are the LLVM + NVVM MLIR, which is the MLIR dialect for LLVM with added GPU instructions.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The two strategies a microarchitecture can use to keep arithmetic units busy in the face of memory latency and dependencies. Instruction-level parallelism mines parallelism from within a single instruction stream; Thread-level parallelism mines it across many. Every modern design is a position on this axis, and the position determines the rest of the microarchitecture.

The choice is not really a choice — it is forced by the workload. ILP works only when the single thread has independent work to find; once dependency chains and branch mispredictions saturate the issue window, no amount of silicon spent on reorder buffers, wakeup logic, or speculation buys more throughput. TLP works only when many independent threads exist; throwing more warps at a single sequential dependency chain accomplishes nothing.
The downstream consequences split everything else:

html:ul.html:li: Control overhead. ILP machines spend most of their area on out-of-order execution machinery — Reorder buffer, Reservation stations, register renaming, CAM-based wakeup-select. TLP machines spend almost none; the schedule is "pick any ready thread."
Caches. ILP needs large caches because a single stalled thread blocks the whole pipeline. TLP can afford small caches because the latency is hidden by switching threads.

Register file. ILP keeps one (renamed) register set hot. TLP keeps every resident thread's register set hot simultaneously — GPU register files are an order of magnitude larger than CPU register files for this reason.
Readiness tracking. ILP pays O(N²) for CAM-based wakeup-select across N in-flight instructions; TLP pays O(1) per instruction via a per-register scoreboard.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The slogan: CPUs spend a lot of silicon to find parallelism in one thread; GPUs spend almost none because they have many threads. Everything downstream — pipeline depth, cache hierarchy, scheduling policy, energy per operation — falls out of which side of this axis the chip sits on.
We define the entropy of a random variable X \sim p_{X}, where p_{X} : \mathcal {X} \to [0, 1] as H(X) = -\sum _{x \in |\mathcal {X}|} p_{X}(x) \log p_{X}(x)
C is said to be convex if for any x_1, x_2 \in C and \lambda \in [0,1] then \lambda x_1 + (1 - \lambda )x_2 \in C

A function f : C \to \mathbb {R} is said to be convex if C is convex, and for any x_1, x_2 \in C, \lambda \in [0,1], we have f(\lambda x_1 + (1 - \lambda )x_2) \leq \lambda f(x_1) + (1 - \lambda )f(x_2) and strictly convex if the inequality is strict.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. This can be thought of as the tangent is always an underestimate of a convex function.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: If f: \mathbb {R}^n \to \mathbb {R} is twice-differentiable, then f is convex if and only if \nabla ^2 f(\boldsymbol {x}) \succeq 0.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. This implies positive curvature or the common "bowl-shaped" interpretation.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: If \phi is a convex function on the range of X then \phi (\mathbb {E}[X]) \leq \mathbb {E}[\phi (X)]

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. When is D_{\mathrm {KL}}(P || Q) = 0?

Answer. Need Q(x) / P(x) to be constant almost surely. This occurs only when Q = P.
Q(x) / P(x) = c \implies Q(x) = P(x) c \iff \sum Q(x) = c \sum P(x) \implies c = 1

fr:mainmatter.html:p: For any pair of random variables, I(X;Y)\geq 0, with equality iff X and Y are independent.

This is Gibb's inequality in disguise: mutual information is the KL divergence between the joint distribution and the product of marginals, I(X;Y)\ =\ D_{\mathrm {KL}}\left (P_{XY}\,\|\,P_X P_Y\right )\ \geq \ 0, and Gibbs' inequality forces every KL divergence to be nonnegative, vanishing exactly when the two arguments coincide — i.e. when P_{XY}=P_XP_Y. It is the root fact from which Conditioning reduces entropy follows.
Observing another variable can only lower entropy on average: H(X\mid Y)\ \leq \ H(X), with equality iff X and Y are independent.

Immediate from Mutual information is nonnegative and the identity I(X;Y)=H(X)-H(X\mid Y): since I(X;Y)\geq 0, the conditional entropy cannot exceed the unconditional one. The caveat is that this holds on average over Y — a particular outcome Y=y can raise the entropy of X; it is only the expectation H(X\mid Y)=\mathbb {E}_Y[H(X\mid Y=y)] that is guaranteed to drop.
The joint entropy of a collection never exceeds the sum of the individual entropies: H(X_1,\dots ,X_n)\ \leq \ \sum _{i=1}^{n} H(X_i), with equality iff the X_i are mutually independent.

Expand the joint entropy by the chain rule, H(X_1,\dots ,X_n)=\sum _i H(X_i\mid X_1,\dots ,X_{i-1}), and apply Conditioning reduces entropy term by term: each H(X_i\mid X_{<i})\leq H(X_i). Conditioning on the past can only shrink each term, so dropping the conditioning gives an upper bound.

Over a finite alphabet \mathcal {X}, entropy is bounded by the log of the alphabet size, H(X)\ \leq \ \log |\mathcal {X}|, with equality iff X is uniform. No distribution on \mathcal {X} is more uncertain than the uniform one.

Again a face of Gibb's inequality: measuring P against the uniform law U(x)=1/|\mathcal {X}|, D_{\mathrm {KL}}(P\,\|\,U)\ =\ \log |\mathcal {X}| - H(X)\ \geq \ 0, so H(X)\leq \log |\mathcal {X}|, and Gibbs' equality condition P=U pins down the maximizer. This is the discrete base case of the general maximum-entropy principle: absent constraints, the flattest distribution wins.
If a_{i},b_{i}>0 for i\in \{1,\ldots ,n\}; A=\sum _{i=1}^{n}a_{i},\quad B=\sum _{i=1}^{n}b_{i} Then, we have: \sum _{i=1}^{n}a_{i}\log a_{i}/b_{i}\geq A\log A/B
Consequences of Log-Sum Inequality. The log-sum inequality upgrades nonnegativity to convexity.

The KL divergence D_{\mathrm {KL}}(P\,\|\,Q) is convex in the pair (P,Q) jointly: for any two pairs and \lambda \in [0,1], D_{\mathrm {KL}}\left (\lambda P_1+(1-\lambda )P_2\ \|\ \lambda Q_1+(1-\lambda )Q_2\right )\ \leq \ \lambda D_{\mathrm {KL}}(P_1\|Q_1)+(1-\lambda )D_{\mathrm {KL}}(P_2\|Q_2).

This drops out of the Log-Sum Inequality applied termwise. Joint convexity is the strong statement: it immediately gives individual convexity in either argument (fix the other and set the two copies equal), and — via D_{\mathrm {KL}}(P\|U)=\log |\mathcal {X}|-H(P) against the fixed uniform law — it makes Entropy is concave a corollary.

Entropy is a concave functional of the distribution: for \lambda \in [0,1], H(\lambda P_1+(1-\lambda )P_2)\ \geq \ \lambda H(P_1)+(1-\lambda )H(P_2). Mixing two distributions never destroys uncertainty — the entropy of a mixture is at least the mixture of entropies.

This is the mirror image of KL divergence is jointly convex. Writing H(P)=\log |\mathcal {X}|-D_{\mathrm {KL}}(P\,\|\,U) against the uniform law U, concavity of H is exactly convexity of P\mapsto D_{\mathrm {KL}}(P\|U). Concavity is what makes the maximum in The uniform distribution maximizes entropy well-behaved — a concave objective on the simplex has a unique maximizer.

fr:title.text: Estimation and the data processing inequality

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Estimation and the data processing inequality

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The information-theoretic reading of a Markov chain: three variables form a chain U\to V\to W when W depends on U only through V, P(W=w\mid U=u, V=v)\ =\ P(W=w\mid V=v). Equivalently, U and W are conditionally independent given V, written (U\perp W)\mid V: P(U=u, W=w\mid V=v)\ =\ P(U=u\mid V=v)\,P(W=w\mid V=v).

Two consequences make this the right object for information theory. The relation is reversible — U\to V\to W \iff W\to V\to U — because conditional independence is symmetric in U and W. And any estimator forms a chain X\to Y\to \hat X: an estimate \hat X is computed from the observation Y alone, never touching X directly. That structure is exactly what the data processing inequality needs — you cannot manufacture information about X by processing Y.
Suppose that U \to V \to W. Then I(U; W) \leq I(U; V).
Intuition. by data processing, we can never create new information about U.

\{X_{n}:n\geq 1\}: a sequence of i.i.d. r.v.s. with mean \mu , variance \sigma ^{2}. Then,
\frac {1}{n}\sum _{i=1}^{n}X_{i}\stackrel {{p}}{{\rightarrow }}\mu

fr:title.text: Weak law of large numbers for functions

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Weak law of large numbers for functions

fr:mainmatter.html:p: \{X_{n}:n\geq 1\}: a sequence of i.i.d. r.v.s. with mean \mu , variance \sigma ^{2}. Let f:\mathcal {X}\rightarrow \mathbb {R} be any function, such that \mathrm {Var}(f(X))=\sigma _{f}^{2}<\infty . Then, \frac {1}{n} \sum _{i = 1}^{n} f(X_i) \stackrel {p}{\rightarrow } \mathbb {E}[f(X)]
Let X_{n}\stackrel {{p}}{{\rightarrow }}X, and f:\mathcal {X}\rightarrow \mathbb {R} denote a continuous function. Then, f(X_n) \stackrel {p}{\rightarrow } f(X)

fr:mainmatter.html:div:
Remark. We have the following important properties
\mathbb {P}(A_{\epsilon }^{(n)})\geq 1-\epsilon {\text { for large enough }}n
|A_{c}^{(n)}|\leq 2^{n(H(X)+\epsilon )}
|A_{\epsilon }^{(n)}|\geq (1-\epsilon )2^{n(H(X)-\epsilon )}\;\text {for large enough }\;n\;, Together, this says that, depending on the entropy, the typical set can contain most of the probability despite being much smaller than the entire space.

html:ul.html:li: Contains almost all the probability.
Consists of \approx 2^{nH(X)}
Almost equiprobable sequences.

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
Let B_{\epsilon }^{(n)}\subset \mathcal {X}^{n} be the smallest set with probability at least 1-\epsilon . Then, we must have
\mathbb {P}(A_{\epsilon }^{(n)}\cap B_{\epsilon }^{(n)})\geq 1-2\epsilon

html:ul.html:li: Rate = \frac {\log _2 \theta }{n}
Probability of decoding error = \mathbb {P}|\hat {X}^n \neq X^n|

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
A positive real number R is said to be achievable if for all \epsilon > 0 (perturbation parameter) there exists a block length N(\epsilon ) such that \forall n > N(\epsilon ) the following holds
There exists an (n, \theta ) coding system such that \frac {\log \theta }{n} < R + \epsilon and \mathbb {P}|\hat {X}^n \neq X^n| < \epsilon .

Alternatively, a positive real number R is said to be achievable if there exists a sequence of coding systems (n, \theta ) such that \lim _{n \to \infty } \frac {\log \theta }{n} \to R and \lim _{n \to \infty } \mathbb {P}|\hat {X}^n \neq X^n| \to 0.
The minimum rate of source coding R_s is the infimum over all R such that R is achievable i.e. R_s \coloneqq \inf \{R \mid R \text { is achievable}\}

html:div.html:p: First we will show that H(X) is achievable. Then we will have R_s \leq H(X) by definition. Idea: take \theta = 2^{n(H(X) + \epsilon )} + 1. Give each element in typical set a unique index, everything else the same index. By properties of typical set, \mathbb {P}(\text {error}) < \epsilon for sufficiently large n.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A coding system (variable rate) with parameter n and an encoder-decoder pair (e,f), where e: \mathcal {X}^n \to \{0, 1\}^*, \quad \text {and} \quad f: \{0, 1\}^* \to \mathcal {X}^n We want the probability of error to be exactly zero; that is \mathbb {P}(X^n \neq f(e(X^n))) = 0.
Let \ell (x^n) denote the number of bits in e(x^n), where e is the encoding function. Then, the rate is \frac {\mathbb {E}[\ell (X^n)]}{n}

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Although it is unclear here, the difference between variable and fixed rate source coding is that we allow our encoder and decoder to map to/from \{0,1\}^* , where ^* is variable i.e. our encoded versions of our alphabet can have varying lengths.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: If e is many-to-one then we say that e is singular. Similarly, if e is one-to-one then e is non-singular.

Given a code with n = 1, we define its extension e^*; that maps \mathcal {X}^* \to \{0,1\}^* by concatenation. That is e^*(x_{1}, x_{2}, \ldots , x_m) = (e(x_{1}), e(x_{2}), \ldots , e(x_m)) A code with encoder e is said to be uniquely decodable if e^* is non-singular.
A code is said to be prefix-free or instantaneous if no codeword is a prefix of another.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Prefix free codes are uniquely decodable. To show unique-decodability, you simply have to provide a way to decode and show it is unique. For prefix-free, greedily decoding suffices.

Remark. Note that non-singular does not imply uniquely decodable. This is because for uniquely decodable we require the extension to be non-singular. For example, if we have that e(0) = 0, e(1) = 1, e(2) = 01. Then e^*(01) = e^*(2). From this, we can see that uniquely decodable is a much stronger condition.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Let e: \mathcal {X} \to \{0,1\}^* denote a prefix-free code with lengths \ell _i = \ell (x_i) for x_i \in \mathcal {X}. Suppose |\mathcal {X}| = m. Then the following must be true: \sum _{i=1}^{m} 2^{-\ell _i} \leq 1
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The converse holds too: for any lengths \ell _i satisfying the inequality, one can construct a prefix-free code whose codewords have exactly those lengths.

Remark. Surprisingly, the constraint is no weaker for the larger class of uniquely decodable codes — every uniquely decodable code must also satisfy the inequality (McMillan). So allowing non-prefix-free-but-decodable codes buys nothing in achievable lengths.

fr:title.text: Performance Limit of Variable Rate Source Codes

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Performance Limit of Variable Rate Source Codes

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A variable rate code with parameter n is a prefix-free code e : \mathcal {X}^n \to \{0, 1\}^* , and a decoder f : \{0,1\}^* \to \mathcal {X}^n

A nonnegative number R is said to be achievable if for all \epsilon > 0, there exists an n(\epsilon ) such that for all n \geq n(\epsilon ), the following holds
There exists a variable rate code with \mathbb {E}[\ell (X^n)]/n = R and \mathbb {P}(X^n \neq f(e(X^n))) = 0 for all n.
The minimum rate of variable rate source coding is R_s \coloneqq \inf \{R \mid R \text { is achievable}\}

fr:title.text: Optimal variable-rate source coding rate equals entropy

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Optimal variable-rate source coding rate equals entropy

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Idea: map symbol x to interval in (0,1) using the CDF of X.

html:ul.html:li: Assume x_{1} < x_{2} < \cdots < x_m
X \sim P_X is \mathcal {X}-valued r.v. with \mathbf {p}_X
CDF of X = F_X(x) \coloneqq \sum _{x_i \leq x} P(x_i)
Midpoint function: G(x) = \sum _{x_i < x} P_X(x) + \frac {P_X(x)}{2} = F_X(x) - \frac {P_X(x)}{2}
Codeword: e(x) = \left \lfloor G(x) \right \rfloor _{\ell (x)}= first \ell (x) bits of G(x)

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
Given x_1, \ldots x_m proceed as follows:
Take two x_i, x_j with smallest p(x_i), p(x_j) append 0 and 1 to their respective codes.
Combine them and treat them as a new value with p(x_i') = p(x_i) + p(x_j)
Repeat

Let X \sim P_X be an \mathcal {X}-valued random variable. Define L_H = \mathbb {E}_{X \sim P_X}[\ell _H(x)] and L^*(P_X) = \min _{\ell _1, \ldots , \ell _m} \sum _{i = 1}^{m} P_X(x_i) \ell _i. Then L_H = L^*(P_X).

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. SFE satisfies L_H < H(X) + \frac {2}{n} i.e. it achieves the optimal rate as n \to \infty . Huffman coding achieves optimality for all finite values n.

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
The "type" \widehat {P}_{x^{n}} associated with x^{n}\in \mathcal {X}^{n} is the probability distribution on \mathcal {X} with pmf \widehat {\boldsymbol {p}}_{x^{n}} , defined as
\widehat {\boldsymbol {p}}_{x^{n}}(a)=\frac {N(a|x^{n})}{n},\quad \text {where}\quad N(a|x^{n}):=\sum _{i=1}^{n}\mathbf {1}_{x_{i}=a}.

For any n\geq 1 , let \mathcal {P}_{n} denote the set of all possible types over the alphabet \mathcal {X} with denominator n . That is,
\mathcal {P}_{n}=\{\widehat {P}_{x^{n}}:x^{n}\in \mathcal {X}^{n}\}.

For any type P\in \mathcal {P}_{n} , we can associate the type class T(P) , defined as
T(P)=\{x^{n}:\tilde {P}_{x^{n}}=P\}.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Example. Let \mathcal {X} = \{1,2,3\}. For n = 2, x^n = 12 the type is (\frac {1}{2},\frac {1}{2}, 0) and the type class is \{12, 21\} .

fr:title.text: The number of types is polynomial in n

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The number of types is polynomial in n

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
Suppose |\mathcal {X}|=m , and \mathcal {P}_{n} denotes the set of all distinct types that can be constructed using elements of \mathcal {X}^{n} . Then, we have_
|\mathcal {P}_{n}|=\binom {n+m-1}{m-1}\leq (n+1)^{m}.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. \mathcal {X}^n has exponentially many elements in n. However, among the exponentially many elements they can only create polynomially many types in n for fixed m.

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
Let P\in \mathcal {P}_{n} denote a type with denominator n . Then, the type class associated with P , defined as T(P)=\{x^{n}:\hat {P}_{x^n}=P\} satisfies
|T(P)|=\prod _{i=1}^{n}\binom {n-N_{i-1}}{N_{i}},\quad \text {where}\quad N_{0}=0,\text {and}\ N_{i}=n\times P(a_{i}),
for all a_{i}\in \mathcal {X}=\{a_{1},\ldots ,a_{m}\} .

fr:title.text: Sequence probability depends only on its type

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Sequence probability depends only on its type

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
Suppose X_{1},X_{2},\ldots ,X_{n}\stackrel {{i.i.d.}}{{\sim }}Q for an arbitrary distribution Q over the alphabet \mathcal {X} . Then, we have
\mathbb {P}(X^{n}=x^{n}) \equiv Q^{n}(x^{n}) = 2^{-n(H(\widehat {P}_{x^{n}})+D_{2n}(\widehat {P}_{x^{n}})=Q)}.
In other words, the probability that X^{n} takes a value x^{n} only depends on x^{n} through its type \widehat {P}_{x^{n}} . Furthermore, types that are far away from Q in relative entropy are exponentially less probable than those that are close to Q .
An immediate consequence of this is that if Q\in \mathcal {P}_{n} , and x^{n}\in T(Q) , then Q^{n}(x^{n})=2^{-nH(Q)} .

Let P\in \mathcal {P}_{n} , and T(P) denote its type class. Then, the following bounds hold:
(n+1)^{-m}2^{nH(P)}\leq |T(P)|\leq 2^{nH(P)}.

fr:title.text: The probability of a type class

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The probability of a type class

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
Let X^{n}=(X_{1},\ldots ,X_{n})\stackrel {{i.i.d.}}{{\sim }}Q for an arbitrary distribution Q on \mathcal {X} . Then, we have the following:
(n+1)^{-m}2^{-nD_{KL}(P|Q)}\leq Q^{n}(T(P))\leq 2^{-nD_{KL}(P|Q)}.
In other words, the probability that X^{n}\stackrel {{i.i.d.}}{{\sim }}Q lies in the type class of P\in \mathcal {P}_{n} is \approx 2^{-nD_{KL}(P||Q)} .

Let X^{n}\sim Q^{n} (i.e., the X 's are drawn i.i.d. from Q ), and E\subset \Delta _{m} denote a subset of pmfs over the finite alphabet X=\{a_{1},\ldots ,a_{m}\} . Let Q^{n}(E) denote the probability \mathbb {P}_{X^{n}\sim Q^{n}}(\widehat {P}_{X^{n}}\in E) . Then, we have
Q^{n}(E)\leq 2^{-nD_{\mathbb {E}}(P^{n} \parallel Q)},
where P^{*}=\inf _{P^{n}\in E}D_{\mathbb {E}}(P^{*}\mid Q) is the closest element of E to Q in terms of relative entropy. Furthermore, if E is the closure of its interior, we also have
\operatorname *{lim}_{n\to \infty }-{\frac {1}{n}}\log Q^{n}(E)=D_{K L}(P^{*}\parallel Q).

A binary hypothesis testing problem involves three components: the data X\sim P , the null hypothesis class \mathcal {P}_{0} , and the alternative distribution class \mathcal {P}_{1} , and the goal is to use the data to decide between the null and alternative; that is,
\text {given}\ X\sim P,\quad \text {decide between}\quad H_{0}:P\in \mathcal {P}_{0},\quad \text {versus}\quad H_{1}:P\in \mathcal {P}_{1}.
When {\cal P}_{0} and {\cal P}_{1} are singleton sets: that is {\cal P}_{0}=\{P_{0}\} and {\cal P}_{1}=\{P_{1}\} with known P_{0},P_{1} , then the problem is called simple hypothesis testing, and this is what we will focus on mostly in this course:
\text {given}\ X\sim P,\quad \text {decide between} \quad H_{0}:P=P_{0},\quad \text {versus}\quad H_{1}:P=P_{1}.

Given \alpha \in (0,1). The optimal hypothesis test in the Neyman-Pearson framework is one that satisfies \operatorname *{min}_{P_{Z\mid X}}\;\mathbb {P}_{H_{1}}\left (Z=0\right ),\quad \mathrm {subject\,to}\quad \mathbb {P}_{H_{0}}\left (Z=1\right )\leq \alpha .

Assume we have prior distributions (\pi _{0}, \pi _{1}) over the two hypotheses and there exists costs C_{1|0}, C_{0|1} associated with type I and type II error respectively. Then the Bayes optimal solution is the solution of \operatorname *{min}_{P_{Z|X}}\pi _{0}C_{1|0}\mathbb {P}_{H_{0}}(Z=1)+\pi _{1}C_{0|1}\mathbb {P}_{H_{1}}(Z=0).

Find a test that minimzes the maximum of the two errors i.e. \operatorname *{min}_{P_{Z|X}}\operatorname *{max}\left \{\mathbb {P}_{H_{0}}(Z=1),\ \mathbb {P}_{H_{1}}(Z=0)\right \}=\operatorname *{min}_{P_{Z|X}}\operatorname *{max}\left \{\pi _{1|0}(P_{Z|X}),\ \pi _{0|1}(P_{Z|X})\right \}.

Let X^{n}\stackrel {{i.i.d.}}{{\sim }}P_{X} , and consider a binary hypothesis testing problem with H_{0}:P_{X}=P_{0} versus H_{1}:P_{X}=P_{1}. Let (\pi _{0},\pi _{1}) denote the prior distribution of the two hypotheses being true. Then, the Bayes optimal hypothesis test with uniform loss is defined as
\Psi _{D}^{*}(X^{n})=\operatorname *{argmin}_{\Psi }\pi _{0}\pi _{10}(\Psi )+\pi _{1}\pi _{01}(\Psi )=\begin {cases}1&\text { if }L(X^{n})>\pi _{0}/\pi _{1}\\ 0&\text {otherwise.}\end {cases},

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter:
In the previous section, we consider the optimal test in the non-asymptotic regime i.e. for finite n what test minimizes the error according to some notion of error. In this section, we ask the question of what is the rate that this error converges to 0. More specifically, if \text {error}_n \leq 2^{-nV} then what is the best (largest) V we can hope to achieve? Kellen Kanarios2025326https://kellenkanarios.com/006G/006G/006G/Optimal Error Exponent in NP settingDefinition
We say that an error exponent V is achievable with type-1 error \alpha >0 , if there exist a sequence of tests (\Psi _{n}) with errors (\alpha _{n},\beta _{n}) , satisfying
\alpha _{n}\leq \alpha ,\quad \text {and}\quad -\frac {1}{n}\log \beta _{n}\geq V.
Let V_{\alpha }:=\sup \{V:V\text { is achievable with type-1 error }\alpha \} . The optimal error exponent is defined as V^{*}=\inf \{V_{\alpha }:\alpha >0\}=\lim _{\alpha \downarrow 0}V_{\alpha } .
Kellen Kanarios2025326https://kellenkanarios.com/006H/006H/006H/Stein's LemmaTheorem
For any \alpha >0 , we have V_{\alpha }=D_{KL}(P_{0}\parallel P_{1}) , which also implies that V^{*}=D_{KL}(P_{0}\parallel P_{1}) . In other words, the relative entropy between P_{0} and P_{1} is the optimal type-II error exponent associated with a binary hypothesis testing problem with H_{0}:P_{X}=P_{0} and H_{1}:P_{X}=P_{1} .
Kellen Kanarios2025326https://kellenkanarios.com/006I/006I/006I/Optimal Bayesian Error ExponentDefinition
Consider a binary Bayesian hypothesis testing problem with prior distribution (\pi _{0},\pi _{1}) . Then, we say that an exponent V>0 is achievable, if there exist a sequence of tests \{\Psi _{n}:n\geq 1\} , with probability of errors \{p_{n}(\Psi _{n})=\pi _{0}\psi _{0}(\Psi _{n})+\pi _{1}\psi _{01}(\Psi _{n}):n\geq 1\} , such that
\liminf _{n\to \infty }\frac {1}{n}\log \left (\frac {1}{p_{n}}\right )=\liminf _{n\to \infty }\frac {1}{n}\log \left (\frac {1}{\pi _{0}\psi _{10}(\Psi _{n})+\pi _{1}\psi _{01}(\Psi _{n})}\right )\geq V.
The optimal Bayesian error exponent for binary hypothesis testing with distributions P_{0} and P_{1} is defined as V^{*}\equiv V^{*}(P_{0},P_{1})=\sup \{V>0:V\text {is achievable}\} .
Kellen Kanarios2025326https://kellenkanarios.com/006J/006J/006J/Optimal Bayesian Error ExponentTheorem
For any \lambda \in [0,1] let P_{\lambda } denote the distribution over \mathcal {X} such that P_{\lambda }(x)\propto P_{0}^{\lambda }(x)\times P_{1}^{\lambda -1}(x) . Let \lambda ^{*}\in [0,1] denote the value at which D_{KL}(P_{\lambda ^{*}}\parallel P_{0})=D_{KL}(P_{\lambda ^{*}}\parallel P_{1}) . Then, the optimal error exponent in the Bayesian hypothesis testing problem with distributions P_{0} and P_{1} satisfies:
V^{*}(P_{0},P_{1})=D_{KL}(P_{\lambda ^{*}}\parallel P_{0})=D_{KL}(P_{\lambda ^{*}}\parallel P_{1}).

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
A sequence of fixed-rate source codes \{(e_{n},f_{n}):n \geq 1\} with parameters \{(n,2^{nR_{n}}):n \geq 1\} is said to be universal with rate R if the following are true:
The limiting rate satisfies \lim _{n\to \infty }R_{n}\leq R .
For any distribution P over the alphabet \mathcal {X} with H(P)<R , the probability of error, \mathbb {P}_{X^{n}\sim P^{n}} \left (f_{n}(e_{n}(X^{n})) \neq X^{n}\right )=0.

fr:title.text: Construction of universal fixed rated code

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Construction of universal fixed rated code

fr:title.text: The type-based fixed-rate code is universal

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The type-based fixed-rate code is universal

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The code constructed above is a universal fixed-rate code, such that \lim _{n\to \infty }\mathbb {P}_{X^{n}\sim P^{n}}(f_{n}(e_{n}(X^{n}))\neq X^{n})=0 for all P with H(P)<R .

fr:title.text: Redundancy of a mismatched code is the KL divergence

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Redundancy of a mismatched code is the KL divergence

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Suppose X\sim Q , but we encode it using an optimal code for P . Then, the redundancy incurred is approximately equal to D_{KL}(Q\parallel P) .

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. It will turn out the optimal choice of Q is the centroid associated with the k elements of \mathcal {P}.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: \mathcal {R}^*(\mathcal {P}) = C(\mathcal {P}) \coloneqq \sup _{\pi \in \Delta _k} I(W; X), where I(W; X) is the mutual information between W \sim \mathbf {\pi } and the output X, with X | (W = i) \sim P_i. The optimal Q^* is Q_{\pi }^* = \sum _{i = 1}^{k} \pi _i^* P_i.

The algorithm proceed as follows:
Initalize the dictionary to an empty set
Make one pass through the stream of data
Add every new "phrase" to the dictionary
Encode only the elements of the dictionary

The channel coding problem has three components — a channel encoder, a noisy channel, and a channel decoder. We pass a message W to the encoder to get X^n, which the noisy channel turns into Y^n; the decoder must recover W from Y^n. The encoder's job is to reduce redundancy while keeping codewords far enough apart that the channel noise cannot confuse them.

A code with parameter (M, n) i.e. (number of messages, number of channel users) consists of an encoder decoder pair
e : [M] \to \mathcal {X}^n
f: \mathcal {Y}^n \to [M]
The rate is \log _{2}\frac {M}{n} = (log of the number of distinct pairs)/(number of channel users)
P_{e, \text {max}} = \max _{1 \leq i \leq m}\mathbb {P}(f(Y^n) \neq i \mid W = i) - worst case over all transmitted messages prob of error.
P_{e} = \frac {1}{M}\sum _{i = 1}^{M} \mathbb {P}(f(Y^n) \neq i \mid W = i) - average prob of error.

We have the following components
\mathcal {X} - input alphabet |\mathcal {X}| < \infty
\mathcal {Y} - output alphabet |\mathcal {Y}| < \infty
P_{Y|X} - transition prob matrix

A rate R \geq 0 is achievable if
\lim _{n\to \infty } P_{e, \text {max}} = 0
\lim _{n \to \infty } \frac {\log _{2}M_n}{n} = R
The channel capacity is the maximum achievable rate i.e. C = \sup \{R \geq 0 \mid R \text { is achievable}\}

fr:mainmatter.html:div:
Remark. We make the following simplifying assumptions (for now)
We assume no feedback. This means that if we have W \to X_{1} \to Y_{1} \to X_{2} \to \cdots then X_i does not use the knowledge of previous channel outputs i.e. Y^{i - 1} \leftrightarrow (W, X^{i - 1}) \leftrightarrow X_i.
Random encoding: X^n(i) is drawn i.i.d from some specified distribution P_X.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The information channel capacity of a DMC (\mathcal {X}, \mathcal {Y}, P_{Y|X}) is defined as C(P_{Y|X}) = \max _{P_X}I(X; Y)
Non rigorous: says that channel capacity and information channel capacity are equal.

A channel is symmetric if
each row is a permutation of the first row
each column is a permutation of the first column
It is weakly symmetric if it satisfies 1 but the columns only sum to the same value.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The capacity of a symmetric or weakly symmetric channel is \log (|\mathcal {Y}|) - H(r), where r is the first row of P_{Y|X}.

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
A channel (with memory) is a sequence of conditional distributions:_
P_{Y_{1}|X_{1}},P_{Y_{2}|Y_{1},X^{2}},P_{Y_{3}|Y^{2},X^{3}},\ldots ,P_{Y_{n}|Y^{n-1},X^{n}},
where P_{Y_{n}|Y^{n-1},X^{n}} is a collection of distributions on \mathcal {Y} one for every (y^{n-1},x^{n})\in \mathcal {Y}^{n-1}\times \mathcal {X}^{n} .

An encoder (with feedback) with parameter (n,\Theta ) for a channel is a vector of mappings e_{i} for i=1,2,\ldots ,n , where
e_{i}:\{1,2,\ldots ,\Theta \}\times \mathcal {Y}^{i-1}\to \mathcal {X}.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. We can decompose the joint distribution P_{X^n, Y^n} in terms of the what depends on the channel and the what depends on the encoder. Namely, P_{X^n, Y^n} = \prod _{i=1}^{n} P_{X_i | X^{i - 1}, Y^{i - 1}} \prod _{i=1}^{n} P_{Y_i | X^{i}, Y^{i - 1}} Note that the first term depends only on the encoder and the second term depends only on the channel.

html:ul.html:li: The encoder is a deterministic function of \mathbf {W}
The channel does not have access to the message \mathbf {W}.

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
The differential entropy h(X) of a continuous random variable X with density f(x) is defined as
h(X)=-\int _{S}f(x)\log f(x)\,dx,
where S is the support set of the random variable.

fr:title.text: Typical Set for Continuous Random Variables

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Typical Set for Continuous Random Variables

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
For \epsilon >0 and any n , we define the typical set A_{\epsilon }^{(n)} with respect to f(x) as follows:
A_{\epsilon }^{(n)}=\left \{(x_{1},x_{2},\ldots ,x_{n})\in S^{n}:\left |-\frac {1}{n}\log f(x_{1},x_{2},\ldots ,x_{n})-h(X)\right |\leq \epsilon \right \},
where f(x_{1},x_{2},\ldots ,x_{n})=\prod _{i=1}^{n}f(x_{i}) .
The volume Vol( A ) of a set A\subset \mathcal {R}^{n} is defined as \text {Vol}(A)=\int _{A}dx_{1}\,dx_{2}\,\cdots \,dx_{n}.

fr:title.text: Properties of the typical set for continuous variables

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Properties of the typical set for continuous variables

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
The typical set A_{\epsilon }^{(n)} has the following properties:
\Pr \left (A_{\epsilon }^{(n)}\right )>1-\epsilon for n sufficiently large.
\mathrm {Vol}\left (A_{\epsilon }^{(n)}\right )\leq 2^{n(h(X)+\epsilon )} for all n .
\mathrm {Vol}\left (A_{\epsilon }^{(n)}\right )\geq (1-\epsilon )2^{n(h(X)-\epsilon )} for n sufficiently large.

For, p_{i}=\int _{i\,\Delta }^{(i+1)\,\Delta }f(x)\,d x=f(x_{i})\Delta \,. We define the discrete approximation of the differential entropy as \begin {align*} H(X^{\Delta })&\coloneqq -\sum _{-\infty }^{\infty }p_{i}\log p_{i} \\ &=-\sum _{-\infty }^{\infty }f(x_{i})\Delta \log (f(x_{i})\Delta ) \\ &=-\sum \Delta f(x_{i})\log f(x_{i})-\sum f(x_{i})\Delta \log \Delta \\ &=-\sum \Delta f(x_{i})\log f(x_{i})-\log \Delta , .\end {align*}

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Taking \Delta = 2^{-n}, then X^{\Delta } is an "n-bit" approximation of X.

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
Let X be any real valued random variable, and let X^{(1/n)} denote the discretization of X with parameter \Delta =1/n . Then, the information dimension associated with X is defined as
d=\lim _{n\to \infty }\frac {H(X^{(1/n)})}{\log n},

A statistic T(X) is sufficient for \theta if the conditional distribution of the data X given T(X) = t does not depend on \theta . Intuitively: if someone hands you T(X), they could regenerate a fresh dataset with the same distribution without knowing \theta — so T lost nothing about \theta .
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Example. Bernoulli. p(\mathbf {x}; \theta ) = \theta ^{\sum _{i} x_i}(1 - \theta )^{n - \sum _{i} x_i}. The distribution only depends on \mathbf {x} through \sum _{i} x_i, so this is a sufficient statistic.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Two accesses to the same memory location, from different threads, where at least one is a write and there is no happens-before relationship between them. The happens-before relation is what synchronization primitives like Locks and atomics induce — when one access happens before another, they are not racing.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Competing for the same lock is not a data race. Whichever thread acquires first happens before the next; the access inside each critical section is sequentially ordered.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Data-race freedom (DRF) is the implicit contract behind every relaxed memory model: if your program has no data races, Relaxed consistency hardware behaves as if it were Sequential consistency. Conversely, in the presence of a data race, the result is undefined by the language and the model.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. Writing data-race-free programs is hard. Treating every shared access as a synchronization point destroys performance.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: SC-preserving compilers approach: forbid the compiler from reordering shared accesses, leaving the hardware free to do TSO-style reordering. The optimization most disrupted is common-subexpression elimination across shared accesses — solved by inserting a "monitor" check on the coherence bit between expressions.

Hardware approach. Treat all pages as local/safe by default and extend page protection bits to the thread level: a non-owning thread accessing the page triggers a fault, exposing the data race at runtime.
A union is a struct in which all members are allocated at the same address. This ensures that the union occupies only as much space as its largest member.
A union can hold a value for only one member at a time.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. No automatic lifetime management for non-trivial types. If a union holds an object with a non-trivial constructor/destructor (like std::string), the compiler won't call them for you. You must manually placement-new and explicitly call the destructor, and getting this wrong causes leaks or corruption. union U { std::string s; int i; }; // destructor not called automatically

fr:mainmatter.html:p: What the CPU does, entirely in hardware, the moment an interrupt arrives — whether a device IRQ, a fault like a Page fault, or a software int N. The point of the hardware path is to land in the kernel at a clean instruction boundary with enough saved state that user execution can resume later as if nothing happened.
Four things happen, in order, with no software involvement:

html:ol.html:li: Reach a clean instruction boundary. The CPU cannot pause halfway through, say, a memory write — architectural state would be inconsistent. So the current instruction either finishes normally (typical for asynchronous interrupts like a timer) or is rolled back as if it never started (faults, where the instruction couldn't complete anyway and needs to be retried). A page-fault handler maps in the missing page and UHNL returns to re-execute the faulting instruction; a timer handler returns to the next instruction.

Save the minimum resume state to the kernel stack. The CPU pushes RIP (where to resume), RSP (the user stack pointer it's about to abandon), the privilege level (CS — were we in ring 3 or ring 0?), and RFLAGS. The save target is the kernel stack, whose address the CPU reads from the Task state segment that the OS set up at boot. This is a security boundary: the kernel never trusts the user stack, since user code could have corrupted it or pointed RSP at something malicious. Note what is not saved: general-purpose registers. The handler itself pushes RAX, RBX, ... if it uses them, which keeps the hardware path fast.

Jump to the handler via the Interrupt descriptor table. The CPU takes the interrupt number N, indexes entry N of the IDT (found via the IDTR register), loads the handler address into RIP, and loads the new code segment, which switches the CPU from ring 3 to ring 0. After this step, the CPU is running kernel code on the kernel stack at the right privilege level — all set up automatically.

Flush the Reorder buffer of work past the interrupt point. In an out-of-order execution CPU the ROB may already contain instructions from after the interrupt — they've been issued speculatively because the front-end ran ahead. Those entries are discarded so the handler sees clean architectural state, as if execution had stopped exactly at the interrupt point. When UHNL returns, those instructions re-execute normally. This is the same squash machinery used for branch mispredictions in Speculative execution — and, famously, the reason Spectre-class side channels exist: speculative work leaves observable traces in caches even after the ROB is flushed.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Once the hardware path finishes, the OS's interrupt handler is running. Its first job is typically to save the remaining registers before doing real work — the rest is software.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Steps 2 and 3 are why an OS needs to set up the IDT and TSS before enabling interrupts. Until both structures point somewhere valid, the first interrupt would either jump to garbage or push to a user-controlled stack — both fatal.
